title: Critical Reasoning: Complete the Argument
prerequisites: identify what an argument is trying to conclude
recognize a supporting vs. undermining statement
learning_objectives: Read the sentence just before the blank to learn what the blank must do.
Follow the connector: 'because/since' needs support; 'but/however' needs a contrast.
Choose the option that logically completes the intended direction of the argument.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do -> we do -> you do

opening.builds_on: Recognizing whether a statement supports or undermines a claim.

items.prompt: After 'because ___,' what must the blank provide?
items.answer: A reason that supports the preceding claim.

items.prompt: After 'the plan will fail, since ___,' what must the blank do?
items.answer: Give a reason the plan will fail.

items.prompt: Does the connector before the blank set the direction?

retrieval_starter.purpose: Recalls that the word before the blank fixes what the completion must accomplish.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: Have them read only the clause containing the blank and predict its job before scanning choices.

questions.prompt: What job does a blank after 'however' do?
questions.answer: It contrasts with or limits the prior statement.

questions.prompt: Why read the clause right before the blank first?
questions.answer: It tells you exactly what the completion must supply.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: Complete-the-argument items are strengthen/weaken in disguise: the connector before the blank ('because,' 'since,' 'but') tells you whether the missing piece must support or push back, and you pick the option that does exactly that.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can use the connector before a blank to choose the option that logically completes the argument in the intended direction.
success_criteria: I can identify the claim the blank must support or oppose.
I can use 'because/since' vs 'but/however' to set the direction.
I can pick the option that fills the blank in that direction.

opening_script.move: Do Now on screen; learners answer from memory, no notes.
Cold-call answers; name the argument's conclusion vs premises.
Bridge to today's question type; state what the stem is really asking.
Move to I do: model the reasoning on one worked item.

i_do.stem: The town plans to reduce car accidents at a dangerous intersection by adding a traffic light. The plan is likely to succeed, because ___. Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.A: most accidents at that intersection are caused by drivers who are unsure when it is their turn to proceed
options.B: the intersection is one of the busiest in the town
options.C: traffic lights are expensive to install and maintain
options.D: the town has added traffic lights at other intersections before
options.E: many drivers in the town exceed the speed limit

i_do.explanation: 'Because' means the blank must give a reason the light will succeed. If accidents stem from confusion over the right of way, a light - which assigns turns - directly fixes that cause. A completes the support.
think_aloud_steps: Conclusion: the traffic-light plan is likely to succeed.
The connector is 'because,' so the blank must SUPPORT that.
A light resolves right-of-way confusion; if that's the cause, the light works.
A supports the plan; the others are neutral or don't explain success.
i_do.key_takeaway: Let the connector before the blank set the direction: 'because/since' needs support, 'but/however' needs a contrast.

we_do.stem: A company believes that offering free coffee will make employees more productive. This belief may be mistaken, however, because ___. Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.A: the main thing slowing employees down is unclear project priorities, which free coffee does nothing to address
options.B: coffee is inexpensive to provide in bulk
options.C: many employees already drink coffee at home
options.D: the office has space for a coffee machine
options.E: some employees prefer tea to coffee

we_do.explanation: 'However ... because' signals the blank must give a reason the belief is mistaken. If the real drag is unclear priorities, free coffee won't help - completing the doubt.
scaffold_hints: The connector 'however' plus 'because' means: give a reason the plan won't work.
What would make free coffee fail to raise productivity?
Pick the choice that names the real obstacle.

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Right - if priorities are the bottleneck, coffee is beside the point.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Cost and preferences don't explain why productivity wouldn't rise.

we_do.stem: A bookstore expects that hosting weekly author events will increase book sales. This expectation is probably well founded, because ___. Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.A: at bookstores that host such events, attendees frequently buy the featured author's books on the spot
options.B: author events require setting up chairs and a microphone
options.C: the bookstore has hosted a few events in the past
options.D: the bookstore is open seven days a week
options.E: some authors are better public speakers than others

we_do.explanation: 'Because' requires support for the expectation. Evidence that attendees buy books at such events directly supports that the events raise sales.
scaffold_hints: The connector is 'because' - the blank must support the expectation.
What fact would show events actually drive sales?
Choose the supporting evidence, not logistics.

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Yes - on-the-spot buying supports the sales expectation.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Setup logistics and hours don't show sales will rise.

you_do.stem: A gym plans to attract more members by opening a swimming pool. The plan is unlikely to work, however, because ___. Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.A: very few people in the area have expressed any interest in swimming, and a large public pool already operates nearby
options.C: the pool will require regular cleaning
options.D: the gym already has a large parking lot
options.E: the gym is busiest in the evenings

you_do.explanation: 'However ... because' needs a reason the plan will fail. Low local interest plus an existing nearby pool means the new pool won't draw members - completing the doubt.

you_do.stem: A city expects that building a new stadium downtown will revive local businesses. This expectation is probably justified, because ___. Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.A: stadium events are projected to bring large crowds who spend money at nearby restaurants and shops before and after games
options.B: the stadium will have a retractable roof
options.C: the city has built stadiums in other neighborhoods
options.D: construction will take about two years
options.E: the stadium will seat tens of thousands of people

you_do.explanation: 'Because' requires support. Crowds spending at nearby businesses is a direct reason local businesses would be revived, completing the argument.

you_do.stem: A manufacturer believes that switching to a cheaper supplier will increase its profit margin. This belief may be unwarranted, however, because ___. Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.A: the cheaper supplier's parts fail more often, and the resulting warranty repairs could cost more than the savings
options.B: the cheaper supplier is located in another country
options.C: the manufacturer has used several suppliers over the years
options.D: the current supplier delivers parts weekly
options.E: the manufacturer sells its products online

you_do.explanation: 'However ... because' needs a reason the belief may fail. If higher failure rates drive up warranty costs beyond the savings, the margin may not improve - completing the doubt.

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.
